title: Virial stress in systems of active Brownian particles in the presence of translational and rotational inertia
authors: Chandranshu Tiwari, Sunil P. Singh, Roland G. Winkler
abstract: We elucidate the stress in a system of active Brownian particles augmented with translational and rotational inertia (ABP+TRI). Stress tensors are derived for periodic systems as well as systems confined between walls by employing Lagrange's equations of motion of the first kind for the rotational motion. Using Langevin simulations of an ideal active gas in two dimensions, we confirm the existence of an equation of state for periodic systems that depends on translational and rotational inertia in general. Confinement implies a strong polarization of the propulsion direction near a wall and an enhanced density, both of which increase with increasing rotational inertia. This affects the local stress tensor normal to the confining walls, leading to a breakdown of the equation of state. Yet the local stress in the bulk part of the confined systems is identical with that of the periodic system. Importantly, for both kinds of boundary conditions, the so-called swim stress is not included in the local stress tensor; thus, in general, the swim stress is not representative of the stress in systems of ABP+TRIs.
summary: This paper rigorously derives the stress tensor for active Brownian particles incorporating both translational and rotational inertia in 2D. It distinguishes between periodic and confined geometries, finding that while an equation of state holds in bulk periodic systems, confinement introduces wall-induced polarization that invalidates simple constitutive relations. The findings emphasize that inertial effects necessitate including angular-velocity contributions beyond just the 'swim stress' to accurately describe mechanical stress.
main_problem: The study elucidates the stress tensor within a system of active Brownian particles augmented with translational and rotational inertia (ABP+TRI). The goal is to understand how these inertial effects modify the mechanical description of activity, especially when comparing periodic versus confined geometries.
main_result: In periodic systems, an equation of state exists for the bulk stress. However, in confined systems, strong wall polarization breaks this simple relationship, and the swim stress alone is insufficient to characterize the total stress tensor due to inertia effects.
method: The authors derive stress tensors using generalized virial theorems applied to the equations of motion (including inertial terms) for both periodic and confined boundary conditions. Langevin simulations are used to validate theoretical predictions across various parameter regimes.
model_or_system: The model consists of $N$ self-propelled point particles whose dynamics include translational damping, rotational damping, and explicit translational ($m$) and rotational ($I$) inertia terms. Interactions involve repulsive Lennard-Jones potentials, and the system is analyzed in 2D under periodic or confined boundary conditions.
key_observables: Stress tensor components ($\sigma^{(k)}, \sigma^{(sm)}, \sigma^{(av)})$, local/global stress tensors, equation of state dependence, particle density profiles, and local polarization $p(x)$.
important_parameters: Translational inertia ($m$), rotational inertia ($I$), Péclet number (Pe), reduced mass ($M$), and reduced moment of inertia ($J$).
main_assumptions: The derivation relies on applying generalized virial theorems, which are complicated by the inclusion of inertial terms. Approximations are used for correlation functions to derive closed-form stress expressions.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the spatial dependence of local stress components ($\sigma_{i,xx}$) near walls in confined systems, showing polarization and density accumulation, and compare global internal stresses between periodic and confined geometries as a function of inertia parameters.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from deriving general stress tensor formulations using virial theorems for inertial active particles. It then contrasts the results obtained under periodic versus confined boundary conditions, analyzing how confinement breaks simple constitutive relations like the equation of state.
why_interesting: This work provides a detailed mechanical description of non-equilibrium matter driven by self-propulsion and inertia, which is crucial for understanding active matter systems relevant to biological or synthetic soft matter physics. The rigorous derivation of stress components beyond simple swim stress is highly valuable.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Orientational correlation function Ce(t) of ABP+TRIs as a function of Drt for various rotational inertia J (legend) in two dimension. The solid lines are the analyt- ical expression Eq. (33)27,43 and the symbols are simulation results. (b) Variation of the active contribution to the mean- square velocity as a function of J for various M. Solid and dashed lines indicate the dependence of Eq. (15) of Ref. 43 and Eq. (38) on J, respectively; bullets are simulation results. The inset shows the relative error between the approximate (Eq. 38) and exact mean-square velocity.

FIG. 2. Virial components σ(k) αα (red), σ(sm) αα (green), and σ(av) αα (blue) of the internal stress σ(i) αα (Eq. (30)) (black squares) in a periodic system for an ideal gas of active Brownian particles with translational and rotational inertia (ABP+TRIs) as a function of the reduced moment of inertia J and the reduced masses (a) M = 0.2, (b) M = 1.0, and (c) M = 10.0 at Pe = 10. σ(id) xx is the swim stress of an ideal ABP gas (Eq. (44)).

FIG. 3. Internal stress tensor, σ(i) αα (Eq. (30)), for a periodic system of an ABP+TRI gas as a function of the reduced mo- ment of inertia J and the reduced masses M = 0.2 (red), 1.0 (green), 5.0 (blue), and 10.0 (magenta) at Pe = 10.

FIG. 4. Internal stress σ(i) αα (Eq. (30)) (black squares) and its components σ(k) αα (red), σ(sm) αα (green), and σ(av) αα (blue) for an ideal gas of ABP+TRIs in a periodic system as a function of the reduced moment of inertia J at Pe = 10. The ratio of the reduced moment of inertia and the reduced mass is fixed at J/M = 2; the x-axis also presents the dependence on M.

FIG. 5. The virial components σ(k) αα (red) and σ(s) αα (green) of the global internal stress σ(i) αα (Eq. (11)) (black squares) in a confined system of ABP+TRIs as a function of the reduced moment of inertia J and the reduced masses (a) M = 0.2 and (b) M = 10.0 at Pe = 10. (c) Global internal (squares) and external (circles) stresses as a function of the reduced moment of inertia and various masses (legend).
